characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: Splinter's owner, a martial artist whose fate at the hands of The Shredder gets the ball rolling. Sometimes he and Splinter are one and the same. Adapted Out: He does not exist in the 2014, 2016, and 2023 movie continuities. Animorphism: Often enough to ato a better known rat identity. Ascended Extra: The 2003 series as himself, and other incarnations where he is also Splinter. As Long as It Sounds Foreign: Hamato is not a Japanese surname. His equivalent in the mangaMutant Turtles, Yoshihama Takeshi, has a much more plausible name.

characters.name: Hamato Yoshi
characters.description: Splinter's owner, a martial artist whose fate at the hands of The Shredder gets the ball rolling. Sometimes he and Splinter are one and the same. Adapted Out: He does not exist in the 2014, 2016, and 2023 movie continuities. Animorphism: Often enough to ato a better known rat identity. Ascended Extra: The 2003 series as himself, and other incarnations where he is also Splinter. As Long as It Sounds Foreign: Hamato is not a Japanese surname. His equivalent in the mangaMutant Turtles, Yoshihama Takeshi, has a much more plausible name.

characters.name: Professor Honeycutt/The Fugitoid
characters.description: A'''fugit'''ive andr'''oid'''. A scientist whose mind was accidentally transferred into a robot's body, and is forced on the run by groups who would use his inventions for their own purposes. Usually a central figure of various "Turtles in Space'' story arcs, and an expert in teleportation technology. Adaptational Late Appearance: If he features at all it's typically after several terrestrial story arcs in most iterations. In the original comics he appeared as early as Issue #5 (not counting his own subseries that actually predates TMNT). Adaptational Species Change: Pre-Fugitoid transformation, he was aHuman Alienin the Mirage comics. In the IDW comics, he's a Neutrino. Then inThe Last Ronin, he's a plain old Earth-born human scientist. Badass Bookworm: Well... his original human form wasn't particularly badass, and even in the original Mirage comics he was an ineffectual wimp even after becoming a robot, but heTook a Level in BadasswhenThe Bus Came Backin Vol 4. In other adaptations, he's generally more badass, or at least Takes that Level a lot earlier owing to having a powerful metallic body at the very least. Brain Uploading: The most commonly accepted explanation for his existence, although doubts are eventually raised as to whether this is the case.

characters.name: Renet
characters.description: An apprentice "Timestress" whose job it is to oversee and protect the timestream. Tends to be the feature character inTime Travelstory arcs. Adaptational Intelligence: Slightly. While Renet is still a bit of aDitzin adaptations, she never starts out asToo Dumb to Livethe way her Mirage incarnation did. Adaptational Late Appearance: Time travel story arcs tend to feature late in most iterations. In the original comics she appeared as early as Issue #8. Adapted Out: The third live action movie prominently features atime travel scepter that looks very similar to Renet's.She herself isn't referenced directly however. Character Development: In fact, we're informed that her parents originally made her take the job as an apprentice timestress in the hope that she would undergo some much-neededCharacter Development. Initially a ditzy, irresponsible and overly impulsive girl, she eventually evolves into a mature, responsible and self-reliant woman — after having spent some time as grim-faced, solemn and reluctant. In the Mirage comics what makes it confusing is that, thanks to her traveling back and forth through time, we don't necessarily see this development in chronological order.

characters.name: The Foot Ninjas
characters.description: An ancient order ofshinobi, assassins and spies dating back to medieval Japan. In present day, they have semi-independent chapters all over the world, overseen by the japanese head council. Conservation of Ninjutsu: Ridiculously prone to this. A horde of Foot Soldiers are going down easy. The four Foot Elite? Maybe an even match for the four Turtles. The lone Shredder? More than a match for the turtles at the same time, unless they've undergone intense training. Cyber Ninja: The Foot Tech Ninja in the 2003 series, which grants them invisibility. Several series have foot-bots that are entirely robotic.In-Universethis makes them more dangerous andrapidly producedthan the human soldiers they replace, but in practice it's so the Turtles can destroy them without censorship. Cycle of Revenge: The Foot are honor-bound toavenge the death of The Shredder. The turtles ever getting a leg up on them usually just means even more are coming. ExpyOf a ninja clan fromMarvel Comicsknow as "The Hand".

characters.name: The Shredder
characters.description: A Ninja Master with ties to the New York underworld, and the franchise's most visible villain. In most incarnations, his name is Oroku Saki and he and Hamato Yoshifought over the same womanuntil his attempt toMurder the Hypotenuseended in disaster, though variations of course exist and the 2003 series notably discards that origin entirely. Adaptational Badass:The first live action film series, 2003 series, and 2012 series versions arefar morecapable and dangerous than the Mirage comics or 1987 series versions of the character.The IDW version was able to put up a fairly decent fight againstBatman(who was wearingPowered Armorat the time) in theBatman/Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtlescrossover.Both the 2003 series and the 2018 series depictthe original Oroku Sakisas demonic entities. The first live action film series, 2003 series, and 2012 series versions arefar morecapable and dangerous than the Mirage comics or 1987 series versions of the character. The IDW version was able to put up a fairly decent fight againstBatman(who was wearingPowered Armorat the time) in theBatman/Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtlescrossover. Both the 2003 series and the 2018 series depictthe original Oroku Sakisas demonic entities.

characters.name: Karai
characters.description: A high-ranking member of the Foot Clan (in Mirage comics she's theleaderof the Foot). Her origin story usually involves her leaving her position in Japan to join the American branch of the Foot in New York, and playing a large role in the City at War arc. Often succeeds Oroku Saki as the Shredder after the turtles (apparently) take him out. May even share a close familial bond with him. Adaptational Heroism:InRise, she is unambiguously heroic, rising up against Shredder when the armor he wore corrupted him and turned the Foot Clan into a gang of terrorists. Adaptational Villainy: Karai in her originalMirageincarnation could best be described as being a neutral character who made a truce with the Turtles but still had her own agenda that she wouldn't reveal to them to maintain the peace. Most subsequent incarnations of Karai in the media, with the exception ofRise, tend to portray her as more of a straight up antagonist serving the Shredder with varying degrees of redeeming traits, and fights between her and the Turtles tend to be more commonplace. Adaptation Relationship Overhaul:In the original Mirage comics, she was the head of the Japanese branch of the Foot clan and had no other connection to the Shredder. In fact, judging by the existence of an adult daughter, she may as well have been older than the Shredder and his superior in ranks. Since the 2003 series, she has been consistently portrayed as the Shredder'sdragon, with the exception ofRise.In the original comics, she has no connections to any of the main characters outside of being part of the Foot clan. In the 2003 series, she is the Shredder's adoptive daughter. The 2012 series also follows suit and portrays her as the Shredder's adoptive daughter, with the added twist that she is Hamato Yoshi's biological daughter. InRise, she is the daughter of Oroku Saki/The Shredder and the ancestor of Hamato Yoshi and the Turtles. InThe Last Ronin, she is the biological daughter of the Shredder. In the original Mirag

characters.name: Bebop and Rocksteady
characters.description: Human gang members who were mutated into a warthog and rhino respectively, and serve as henchmen to the Shredder. Neither of them are particularly bright, but they're well armed, muscular, and always seem tobounce back from a defeat. Adaptational Badass: Series with less restrictions on violence tend to show Bebop and Rocksteady as being much more dangerous than the original 1987 cartoon did.In theIDW Comics, they're still dunces, but they'rebloodthirstydunces withSuper-StrengthandSuper-Toughness. They can literally rip human opponents limb from limb, and shrug off being shot with high explosives.The2012 Cartoonmakes them smarter and more competent than their 1987 counterparts, as well as giving them more useful technology, such as Bebop gaining an invisibility suit. In theIDW Comics, they're still dunces, but they'rebloodthirstydunces withSuper-StrengthandSuper-Toughness. They can literally rip human opponents limb from limb, and shrug off being shot with high explosives. The2012 Cartoonmakes them smarter and more competent than their 1987 counterparts, as well as giving them more useful technology, such as Bebop gaining an invisibility suit. Adaptation Name Change: It's unclear where their names came from in the '87 series but they had no other moniker. Other incarnations give them human names, with Bebop and Rocksteady being their gang or mutant nicknames.Bebop's real name was Anton Zeck in the 2012 series, and Genealdo or Gene in the IDW series.Rocksteady's real name was Ivan Steranko in the 2012 series, Maxence or Max in the IDW series, and OwenRocksteedin the 2014 film series (withIvan as another criminal alias).

characters.name: Foot Elite
characters.description: Elite ninjas of the Foot and the Shredder's personal body guards and lieutenants. All Asians Wear Conical Straw Hats: Consistently wear Japanese rice hats across all incarnations. Avenging the Villain: After the Shredders death at the hands of the Turtles, the remaining Elites swear a vendetta against both them as the Foot now fight for control over New York. Elite Mook: In all versions, they serve as the absolute best fighters of the Foot, second only to Shredder himself (and othernamed characters). Enemy Civil War: After Oroku Saki dies they strike at all parties involved in the war over the New York underworld, including the Japanese Foot. They are often enough rivals to Karai's claim over the Foot Clan in the initial power vacuum. Also rivals to the Demon Shredder in the 2003 series being loyal to the Utrom Shredder and (by that point) Karai instead.

characters.name: Shredder Mutants
characters.description: Hideously mutated clones of The Shredder, dressed in modified Shredder armor and trained to work as elite Foot soldiers. All There in the Manual: They are never named in the comics or the TV shows. Their names in the 4Kids websites and the toys are also different. The four-armed Shredder is called Multi-Arm Shredder in toy form, and Shiva Shredder on 4Kids' website, the one with large, monstrous crustacean-like claws is known as Claw Shredder in toy form, and Spike-Armed Shredder on 4Kids' website. The diminutive Shredder with a single, long wrist blade on each arm is called Mini Shredder on 4Kids' website. Big, Thin, Short Trio: Multi-Arm Shredder is huge and hulking, Claw is thin and lean and Mini Shredder is short. Body Horror: BIG time. Its probably a good thing they are never seen without masks.Claw takes it an extra notch, he doesn't even have HANDS, his arms are horrific, lumped pincer claws. Claw takes it an extra notch, he doesn't even have HANDS, his arms are horrific, lumped pincer claws.

characters.name: Krang
characters.description: An alien general fighting a losing war, and who turns to Earth for assistance. A small, brain-like creature, he often uses a robotic exoskeleton to move around. His suit is often distinguished by its sunglasses-like visor, attenae, or yellow and red color scheme, if not just being a battle mech. The original Krang wasnota TCRI alien, but in later appearances The Krang would either an evil sect of them or an outright replacement leaving Utroms unmentioned. Adaptation Species Change: He was introduced in the 1987 series asa disembodied brain of an alien warlordwho visually resembled the Utroms from the Mirage comics. The Archie comics followed the same backstory. In the 2003 series, a random Utrom named Krang makes a cameo as a throwawayJoke Character. The 2012 series madethe Kraangthe same species as the Utroms, whileRisemade them a squid-like alien race. The 2012 show's crossover episode with the 1987 seriesretconnedthe original Krang to be another Utrom. Krang is an Utrom in the IDW comics as well. While the Platinum Dunes movie does not give Krang any backstory so it is unknown if he is an Utrom or has a unique backstory. Big Bad: Plays this role in most versions he appears in, sometimes with Shredder like in the Archie and 1987 series or on his own like the 2012 series and IDW. Bizarre Alien Biology: His brain, having been scooped out of its original skull, can survive perfectly well on its own in the open air and has primitive arms (tentacles), eyes and a fang-filled mouth in its own right.In IDW, its revealed Utroms are a parasitic race when Leatherhead devours him whole, only to regrow alive and well on the poor croc's chest. In IDW, its revealed Utroms are a parasitic race when Leatherhead devours him whole, only to regrow alive and well on the poor croc's chest.

characters.name: Rock Soldiers
characters.description: A race of stone humanoids, who work for Krang. Canon Foreigner: Created for the 1987 show. Mooks: Of Krangs's army. Proud Warrior Race: More like a proud soldier race, as their entire culture is about the military and discipline, and they are in a strict hierarchy. Rock Monster: They are all made from stone.

characters.name: Tragg and Granitor
characters.description: Two rock soldiers, who act as the army's leaders. Adaptational Badass: In most incarnations, they are as tall as humans and use weapons to attack. In the 2012 series, they are gigantic and have a magma body.While not to such degree, they are also more capable and dangerous in the IDW comics. While not to such degree, they are also more capable and dangerous in the IDW comics. King Mook: The biggest and toughest of the Rock Soldiers. Mook Lieutenant: The miliary commanders of the other Rock Soldiers.

characters.name: Baxter Stockman
characters.description: Mad Scientistand creator of the mouser robots. Never quite seems to remain whole (or even human, in some versions). Adaptation Origin Connection:Inthe 2016 film, he is the one who mutated Bebop and Rocksteady.Inthe 2023 film, he is the one who created the Ooze and dumped it into the sewers, meaning he is directly responsible for the mutation of the Turtles, Splinter, Bebop, Rocksteady, Leatherhead and the other mutants. Inthe 2016 film, he is the one who mutated Bebop and Rocksteady. Inthe 2023 film, he is the one who created the Ooze and dumped it into the sewers, meaning he is directly responsible for the mutation of the Turtles, Splinter, Bebop, Rocksteady, Leatherhead and the other mutants. Adaptation Relationship Overhaul: In Mirage, he's a truly independent antagonist who doesn't have connections with the Shredder. In many subsequent adaptations Stockman is made into a henchman of the Shredder or the Utroms,though he may still have his own ambitions.

characters.name: The Rat King
characters.description: A man dressed up in rags and bandages who shares a rapport with rats. Varies wildly within incarnations—descriptions for each can be seen inthe other Wiki. Adaptational Badass: Depending on the incarnation, the Rat King can be either a man or he can be something more than a man with more superhuman attributes. However, in the IDW series, the Rat King is basically a god-like being amongst a pantheon of deities. Adaptational Wimp: The version of the Rat King seen inTeenage Mutant Ninja Turtles & Other Strangeness, being based off of the character's earliest appearances in the Mirage comicsnoteTales of the TMNT #4, is the most mundane and down-to-earth incarnation of them all; he's just a crazy homeless person. He doesn't even have the"rat controller"gimmick of his other incarnations; he's just delusional and insists he's a monster. Ascended Extra: He was aMonster of the Weekwhodiedin his first appearance. Didn't stop him from becoming one of the Turtles' most prominent adversaries. He appeared in the '87 series just a year after his debut in the Mirage comics and the rest was history. Badass Longcoat: Wears one during his single appearance in the Red Sky seasons, "Wrath of the Rat King".

characters.name: Agent Bishop
characters.description: A chief agent of the Earth Protection Force. He seeks to eliminate the threat of mutants and aliens in New York and around the world making him at odds with the titular mutant heroes. Adaptational Personality Change: The original Bishop was fairlycalm and composed, and the 2012 series he's stoic as a Norman Kraang. In IDW he's far more deranged and prone to rambling, and in Mutant Mayhem she's much moreHot-Blooded. Adaptational Wimp:In the IDW comics hehas a severe deformity that leaves him helpless without aMobile Human Suit.In the Mutant Mayhem series she's more clearly middle aged rather than in fighting prime. In the IDW comics hehas a severe deformity that leaves him helpless without aMobile Human Suit. In the Mutant Mayhem series she's more clearly middle aged rather than in fighting prime.

characters.name: Slash
characters.description: AnEvil Counterpart(Though usually moreAnti-Hero) humanoid turtle, often based on a snapping turtle. His origins have varied greatly across continuities, being either a mutant of varying origins, or an alien. Aside from appearing as a twisted knockoff of the Turtles, another common feature is a fondness for palm trees. Anti-Hero: In most continuities, he tends to be this rather than an out-and-out villain, though he usually starts out as an antagonist to the Turtles. The Berserker: He makes Raphael seem mild and gentle in comparison. Brought to You by the Letter "S": He often wears a belt with a backwards "S" on the buckle. In the "Back to the Sewer" season of the 2003 series, Hun wears a similar belt with a similarly shaped Dragon and then inTurtles Forever,he mutates into Slash. Canon Character All Along: At least twiceIn the grand finale of the '03, original character Hun (a breakout in his own right by the point after 7 seasons of character development) ends the series mutanting into a humanoid turtle. After all his various costume changes, the one he's in at the time makes him look like Slash.A more direct set up is Raph's pet turtle Spike in the 2012 series which, is by all accounts treated like a genuineFurry ConfusionTeam Petin season 1, eventually gets hit with mutagen and changes his name to Slash in season 2.

characters.name: Mondo Gecko
characters.description: A skateboarding gecko who is friends with Michelangelo Heel–Face Turn: In the 1987 and 2012 series, he used to be a criminal working under Mr. X. Lovable Lizard: Friendly and easygoing in most incarnations. Any incarnation of Mondo that start of as antagonistic won't stay that way for long. Metamorphosis: Gecko to anthro gecko (1987 series and IDW comics), human to anthro gecko (Archie comics and 2012 series). Non-Mammalian Hair: Except for the 2012 series.

characters.name: Tokka and Rahzar
characters.description: A snapping turtle (Tokka) and wolf (Rahzar) mutated by the Shredder. Adaptational Badass: Rahzar's 2012 incarnation is much faster and more intelligent than how he usually is portrayed. And Tokka is an unrelated giantkaijumonster. Adaptational Intelligence: In the 1987 animated series, they are a lot more intelligent and eloquent than their live action movie counterparts. Adaptational Species Change: InTurtles Forever, they are human Foot Ninja mutated into humanoid animals, like Bebop and Rocksteady, instead of animals mutated into humanoid forms. In the 2012 version, Tokka is an alienkaiju. Advertised Extra: They appear on the poster ofTurtles Foreverbut are left unnamed and largely relegated to cameos in the film itself.

characters.name: Wingnut and Screwloose
characters.description: A bat and mosquito duo who are either aliens or mutants. This is the only consistent thing about them (and it's not quite consistent). Author Appeal: The Archie versions have strong environmentalist themes, same as many of the works by writers Stephen Murphy and Ryan Brown. Canon Foreigner: Wingnut debuted in a cereal tie in comic of all things the year before he properly appeared in the Archie comics (partnered with Screwloose), and two years before appearing in the '87 series. Satellite Character: Wingnut has made a number of appearances on his own in the video games and some continuities, but Screwloose never appears solo. Even in their first toy, Screwloose was a solid yellow-colored accessory while Wingnut was the fully-painted main feature. Sole Survivor: In the Archie canon, they're the only survivors of two species that were exterminated by Krang.

characters.name: The Punk Frogs
characters.description: A group of mutant frogs that may oppose the turtles before befriending them. Adapted Out: Only Ghengis appears in Mutant Mayhem, without his brothers. Canon Foreigner: Created for the '87 series. They would gain even more members in the IDW comics than the usual four (including the first true iteration of Venus after the much malignedNext Mutationseries). Heel–Face Turn:In the '87 show the were created by Shredder to destroy the turtles, but relented after they realized the turtles weren't so bad.In 2012 they wanted to wage war against humans, but Mike and Napoleon eventually made peace with them.In Mutant Mayhem it's just Ghengis who is part of Superfly's gang before they mellow out and become the Mutanimals. In the '87 show the were created by Shredder to destroy the turtles, but relented after they realized the turtles weren't so bad.

characters.name: Pigeon Pete
characters.description: A mutant Pigeon that befriends Michelangelo and the rest of the turtles. Cloudcuckoolander: Even more foolish and unpredictable than Mikey. Sometimes he still acts like a full on pigeon despite his human sized body. Lost in Imitation: While he had his breakout role as a mutant pigeon in the 2012 series, the '87 show had Mike befriend a regular pigeon named Pete that never mutated. Metamorphosis: From pigeon to pigeon man. He keeps his animal mentality more than most mutants.

characters.name: Lord Dregg
characters.description: An alien crime lord, who has an enmity towards the Turtles. Adaptational Species Change: In the 1987 show, he was a humanoid alien, while in the 2012 show he's an insectoid one. Big Bad: Becomes the main villain in the 1987 series during the "Red Sky" seasons. Composite Character: In the 2012 show, he is combined with Maligna, a leader of an insectoid race from the Archie comics. Knight of Cerebus: In the 1987 series, he's much more serious compared to the version's Shredder, rarely having any comedic moments and in general is much more serious.
